story:
Inquisitor Gregor Ezekyle Kern was having a bad day. The witch he’d been hunting slipped away. His hired help got cut down by a stranger, who then came for Kern—and died for it. Minor setback. Kern had fought tougher foes. He followed the witch’s tracks into a wet, miserable forest.

He found her in a clearing by a moss-covered stump, speaking with a tall man who carried himself like a warrior. Behind them, a hole wide enough to walk through gaped in the side of a hill. A stash for her tools, Kern guessed. He waited in the brush and checked the ancient book he’d dragged through the mud. The ink had bled to nothing. Useless. He tossed it aside. Old-fashioned way, then.

The man said something she didn’t like. Her face went white. He grabbed her arm and tugged hard. Kern burst through the brush. The man froze. Good. Kern drew his rapier and advanced, smiling.

“By order of the Inquisition, release this woman and no harm will come to you.” A lie. The Grand Master’s decree was clear: kill the witch and anyone aiding her. Kern didn’t mind. Every foe was a problem to solve—by solved he meant killed. This man counted.

The warrior stepped between Kern and the witch. Up close, his face looked a lot like hers. Brother, maybe. Or cousin.

“No,” the man boomed. “This ends here, inquisitor. I have orders to take this woman before the Council.”

Kern had orders too—signed by both Council and Inquisition—calling for her head. She’d killed five inquisitors, breaking the truce with non-evil casters. Either she was strong, or those inquisitors were weak. The Inquisition didn’t hire weak. Looking at this warrior, Kern knew she wasn’t alone. The decree named her a druid. Labels didn’t matter. Magic was a blight to be stamped out.

“Who gave those orders?” he asked, playing along.

“Lord Ventillius. High Councilor.”

“Your papers?”

The man’s eyes narrowed. “Right here.”

His hand went to his jacket. Kern spotted the jade necklace at his throat. Too late.

The man lunged and changed mid-air into a massive wolf. Kern ducked. The beast sailed over him and landed behind. The witch bolted for the dark hole in the hill.

Not again.

Kern turned. The wolf paced, low growl rolling out between bared teeth.

“You stupid fool.” It had been a while since he fought a shape-shifter. “So be it.”

The wolf sprang. Kern sidestepped and kicked hard, boot striking its snout with a crack. The wolf yelped back, nostrils flaring. Kern brought his blade down, but the wolf covered the ground too fast, clamping its jaws onto his forearm. Pain flared. He smashed its nose and skull with his free fist until the rapier slipped from his blood-slick grip.

No time to reach it. He dropped to a knee and yanked the dagger from his boot. The wolf barreled into him again, teeth finding the same arm. Kern roared and drove the dagger into the side of its head.

The pressure vanished. The wolf slumped and folded back into a man, staring sightlessly at the sky. Kern planted a boot on the dead man’s face and slid the blade free. He wiped it clean, sheathed it, and recovered his rapier.

Bleeding and tired of the witch’s helpers, he gripped his ruined arm and walked toward the hill’s mouth, ready for whatever waited in the dark.
